does: This paper introduces the Unlearning Depth Score (UDS), a mechanistic metric for evaluating whether knowledge has been truly erased from large language models during unlearning procedures. The method uses activation patching to identify which layers encode target knowledge by comparing a retain model baseline against unlearned models, then quantifies erasure on a 0-1 scale. The authors conduct a comprehensive meta-evaluation across 20 existing metrics applied to 150 unlearned models produced by 8 different unlearning methods, demonstrating that UDS achieves superior faithfulness and robustness compared to output-level alternatives that fail to detect residual knowledge in internal representations.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that output-level evaluation metrics for unlearning are insufficient, extending recent white-box mechanistic approaches that examine internal model representations. It responds to limitations in prior studies that either require auxiliary training procedures or dataset-specific adaptations, addressing the broader challenge of reliable auditing in unlearning research. The paper connects to activation patching and causal tracing techniques from mechanistic interpretability literature, applying these tools to the specific problem of verifying knowledge erasure, while engaging with the expanding body of post-hoc unlearning methods developed for privacy and safety applications.
enables: This work establishes a generalizable evaluation framework that can assess unlearning quality across different methods and contexts, potentially raising standards for future unlearning research by providing a reliable auditing mechanism. By revealing layer-level disagreements among metrics, UDS enables deeper investigation into how unlearning actually modifies model internals, pointing toward more targeted unlearning approaches that operate at specific mechanistic depths. The metric framework opens directions for understanding which unlearning methods achieve genuine erasure versus superficial obfuscation, and could support development of unlearning techniques designed to minimize residual knowledge across all representational levels.
